title: Today's Tech Brief: 3D-printable humanoid legs let robotics experiments run wild
summary: A look at the latest tech news, including 3D-printable humanoid legs and American Airlines' new Wi-Fi upgrade.
imageAlt: Image from source article for 3D-printable humanoid legs let robotics experiments run wild
imageCaption: 3D-printable humanoid legs let robotics experiments run wild
imageSourceTitle: 3D-printable humanoid legs let robotics experiments run wild

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's tech brief. I've got a couple of interesting stories to share with you. First up, a $2,500 pair of humanoid robot legs has been making waves in the robotics world. These legs are built from 3D-printed parts and off-the-shelf components, making them relatively affordable and accessible to researchers and hobbyists. I mean, who wouldn't want to experiment with robot legs, right?

According to a report from Ars Technica, these 3D-printable humanoid legs are allowing robotics experiments to run wild. The legs are designed to be highly customizable, allowing researchers to test out different configurations and control systems. This kind of flexibility is a game-changer for the field of robotics, as it enables researchers to rapidly prototype and test new ideas. It's like having a robotics playground, and I'm excited to see what kind of creative projects come out of this.

Also in the news, American Airlines is upgrading its Wi-Fi game with Starlink, a high-speed internet service developed by SpaceX. Starting next year, the airline will begin installing Starlink on its Airbus fleet, providing passengers with fast and reliable internet access during their flights. I've had my fair share of frustrating in-flight Wi-Fi, so I'm looking forward to this upgrade. No more buffering on my in-flight Netflix binge-watching sessions.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
